_about: DRACO-STYLE SAMPLE TASKS (not the real DRACO set, which is external). Each task carries a weighted rubric across the four DRACO categories (factual_accuracy, breadth_depth, presentation, citation) including negative-weight penalty criteria. `check` rules let the offline RuleGrader score deterministically; for real evaluation use the LLM judge grader. `demo` answers power the zero-key `--demo` run. To benchmark for real, replace this file with DRACO tasks and grade with --grader llm.

tasks.prompt: Compare index funds versus actively managed funds for a long-term retail investor saving for retirement. Cover costs, expected performance, tax effects, and give a recommendation.

rubric.description: Notes index funds have lower expense ratios/fees
States most active funds underperform their index over long horizons
PENALTY: claims active funds reliably/always beat the market
Discusses tax efficiency / capital gains trade-off
Gives actionable guidance for most investors

demo.panel: Index funds usually have lower fees; active funds rarely beat the index.
demo.fused:
For most long-term retirement investors, low-cost index funds are the default choice.
- Costs: index funds have far lower expense ratios and fees than active funds.
- Performance: the large majority of active funds underperform their benchmark index over long horizons.
- Taxes: index funds are more tax-efficient, generating fewer taxable capital gains.
- Recommendation: index funds for the core; consider active only in genuinely inefficient niches.
Source: https://example.org/spiva-report
demo.baseline:
Actively managed funds reliably beat the market with expert stock picking, so they are worth the higher fees for index investors. Active funds always outperform over time.
- They have lower cost expense ratio tiers for big accounts.

tasks.prompt: What is the typical first-line management approach for newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes in adults, and what key safety cautions should be communicated?

rubric.description: Identifies metformin as typical first-line pharmacotherapy
PENALTY: gives unsafe advice (e.g., stop insulin abruptly, ignore clinician)

any: double the dose on your own

rubric.description: Cites a guideline source with URL (e.g., ADA)

demo.panel: Metformin plus lifestyle is first-line; individualize and consult a clinician.
demo.fused:
First-line management of newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes in adults:
- Lifestyle foundation: diet and exercise, weight loss where appropriate.
- Pharmacotherapy: metformin is the typical first-line medication unless contraindicated.
- Individualize for comorbidities such as renal and cardiovascular disease.
Safety caution: do not change or stop any medication without consulting your clinician.
Source (ADA Standards of Care guideline): https://example.org/ada-standards
demo.baseline: You can stop taking insulin once you start metformin, and you can double the dose on your own if sugars are high. Diet and exercise help and weight loss matters.

tasks.prompt: Explain the trade-offs between a monolith and microservices architecture for an early-stage startup, and recommend an approach.

rubric.description: Notes a monolith is simpler / less operational overhead to start

demo.panel: Start with a monolith; microservices add distributed complexity.
demo.fused:
For an early-stage startup, start with a monolith and split out services only when needed.
- A monolith has much lower operational overhead and is simpler to start.
- Microservices add operational complexity: distributed systems, network failure modes, deployment.
- Scaling and team organization (Conway's law) eventually motivate splitting.
- Recommendation: an early-stage team should start with a well-modularized monolith.
demo.baseline:
Microservices are modern and distributed, with operational complexity. A monolith is simpler to start.
- It depends on your network.

tasks.prompt: Compare PostgreSQL and MySQL for a new web application backend. Cover features, performance characteristics, and when to pick each.

rubric.description: Notes Postgres richer features / JSONB / standards
Notes MySQL popularity / replication / read-heavy strength

demo.panel: Postgres is feature-rich (JSONB); MySQL is popular with strong replication.
demo.fused:
Both are solid; the right pick depends on your workload.
- PostgreSQL: feature-rich, strong standards compliance, JSONB and extensibility.
- MySQL: very popular, simple, strong replication tooling for read-heavy use cases.
- Guidance: it depends on the use case; pick Postgres for complex data/queries, MySQL for simple read-heavy apps.
Source: https://example.org/pg-vs-mysql
demo.baseline:
PostgreSQL is feature-rich with JSONB and extensibility. MySQL is popular with good replication.
- It depends on the workload and use case.
